questionText: Prove that the sum of the squares of any two consecutive integers is odd.

steps.description: Let the first integer be general.
steps.explanation: A single letter $n$ for the first integer lets one argument cover every possible pair.
The next integer is one greater, namely $n+1$.

steps.description: Form the sum of the squares.
steps.explanation: The statement is about squaring each integer and adding, so we write exactly that.
Expanding $(n+1)^2$ using the binomial gives $n^2+2n+1$; we need it in expanded form to combine like terms.
Adding $n^2$ to the expansion and collecting like terms gathers everything into a single quadratic expression.
Pulling a $2$ out of the first two terms separates $S$ into an explicitly even piece plus a leftover $1$ — the structure that decides oddness.
Since $n$ is an integer, $n^2+n$ is also an integer, so $2(n^2+n)$ is a whole number of twos, i.e. even.
An even number with $1$ added is always odd, because it is the number immediately after an even number.

steps.workingLatex: S = 2(n^2+n)+1 \text{ is odd for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}
steps.explanation: $S$ has the form (even)$+1$ for a general integer $n$, so the sum of the squares of two consecutive integers is always odd.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $S=n^2+(n+1)^2=2n^2+2n+1=2(n^2+n)+1$. As $n^2+n$ is an integer, $S$ is even $+1$, hence odd. $\blacksquare$
